```text
  result structs, Blueprint-callable preview functions, and P20/P13 adapters.

P23S-003 Footstep and impact matrix first slice
  Add Stone, Dirt, Mud, Wood, Metal, Flesh, Water, Rot, and Snow coverage for
  walk/run/roll/land footsteps plus slash/projectile impact fixtures.

P23S-004 Weather material response bridge
  Add Material Parameter Collection profile routing for rain, snow, ash, and dry
  transitions, plus screenshot/evidence fixtures for UDS/weather profiles.

P23S-005 Environmental hazard profile and damage bridge
  Add rot pool and fall kill volume profiles, P13 damage/status routing,
  AI pathing policy, save/load policy, and death-loop anchor checks.

P23S-006 Designer labs and audit commandlets
  Add Surface Response Lab, Footstep And Impact Matrix, Weather Material
  Response Lab, Hazard Scenario Lab, and commandlet reports.

P23S-007 Dashboard and package gates
  Add dashboard cards and package blockers for missing response profiles,
  default surface leaks, invalid hazard damage routes, and missing accessibility
  variants.
```

Recommended sequencing:

1. Build P23S-001 before expanding Landscry/PCG terrain output.
2. Build P23S-002 and P23S-003 before adding more weapon families or footstep
   notifies.
3. Build P23S-004 before UDS/weather presets become zone defaults.
4. Build P23S-005 before The Below or open-world hazards become progression
   blockers.
5. Build P23S-006 and P23S-007 once the first matrix can be trusted by artists,
   designers, and QA.

### 23.17 Third-party asset, plugin, license, provenance, and upgrade governance package

Ascentia will use third-party assets and tools. That is not a weakness; it is
normal production leverage. The risk is letting those packs become invisible
architecture. Game Animation Sample, Essential Bow, AssassinGirl, Ultra Dynamic
Sky, marketplace animation packs, sound libraries, generated meshes, UI skins,
Fab art kits, plugins, and future mocap/audio/vendor content need explicit
intake, license notes, technical risk, replacement plans, and upgrade evidence.

This section is production governance, not legal advice. The binding license is
always the actual vendor/license text and purchase record. The Ascentia rule is
that no external asset or plugin reaches production without a visible source
record, approved use case, technical audit, license summary, and owner.

Primary references:

- Fab's Standard License summary says assets may be used commercially or
  privately, modified into projects, commercially distributed as incorporated
  project content, used with compatible tools, and shared with collaborators on
  the project; it also says assets may not be redistributed standalone.
  https://www.fab.com/eula
- Fab license documentation lists Creative Commons Attribution and Standard
  license types, explains Personal/Professional tiers, and calls out the NoAI
  meta tag conflict with CC-BY distribution.
  https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/fab/licenses-and-pricing-in-fab
- Unreal plugins are discovered from `.uplugin` descriptor files, and plugins
  with `Source` folders are compiled by UnrealBuildTool with the game project.
  https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/plugins-in-unreal-engine
- Unreal modules are the basic code organization unit for editor tools,
  runtime features, libraries, and other functionality. Plugin and module
  boundaries should therefore be treated as production architecture, not just
  install state.
  https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/unreal-engine-modules
- `FPluginDescriptor` contains plugin metadata such as version, engine version,
  modules, marketplace URL, docs URL, support URL, target platforms, content
  support, and dependency plugin references. Ascentia can audit these fields.
  https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/API/Runtime/Projects/FPluginDescriptor
- `FModuleDescriptor` exposes module compatibility, build/load checks, loading
  phases, and module read/write/update behavior. Module drift is a real package
  and editor-startup risk.
  https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/API/Runtime/Projects/FModuleDescriptor

Runtime module layout:

```text
Source/Ascentia/Public/ContentGovernance/AscentiaExternalContentTypes.h
Source/Ascentia/Public/ContentGovernance/AscentiaVendorLicenseTypes.h
Source/Ascentia/Public/ContentGovernance/AscentiaPluginRiskTypes.h
Source/Ascentia/Public/ContentGovernance/AscentiaContentProvenanceTypes.h
```

```text
  AllowsGeneratedDerivativeUse

FAscentiaExternalAssetProvenance

  bModified

  bContainsHumanLikenessOrVoice
  bContainsTrademarkOrLogo
  bContainsMusicOrLicensedAudio
  bRequiresCredit
  EvidencePath

FAscentiaThirdPartyPluginProfile
  PluginName
  PluginDescriptorPath
  PluginKind: Engine, Project, Marketplace, Fab, EpicSample, Internal,
    ExperimentalEngine, ExternalSource
  RuntimeRisk: None, Low, Medium, High, Blocker
  EditorRisk: None, Low, Medium, High, Blocker
  ShippingPolicy
  ExpectedEngineVersion
  ExpectedPluginVersion
  ExpectedModules
  ExpectedDependencies
  ExpectedTargetPlatforms
  MarketplaceUrl
  DocsUrl
  SupportUrl

FAscentiaExternalContentApproval

  RequestedUse
  SystemTags

  ApprovalState

FAscentiaExternalUpgradePlan

```

Runtime boundary:

```text
UAscentiaExternalContentRegistrySubsystem : UGameInstanceSubsystem
  ResolveExternalContentSource(SourceId)
  QueryAssetProvenance(AssetPath)
  QueryPluginRisk(PluginName)
  IsExternalContentAllowedInBuild(SourceId, BuildProfile)
  EmitExternalContentEvidence(Result)
```

Rules:

- External content starts in staging or quarantine, not `/Game/Ascentia`
  production folders.
- Every promoted asset has a source record. "Downloaded from somewhere" is a
  blocker.
- The tool records a license summary for production visibility, but the source
  license text/purchase record remains the authority.
- Fab Standard assets can be incorporated into the game under the license, but
  Ascentia must never expose raw/source assets as standalone redistributable
  content.
- CC-BY content requires attribution planning. Do not mix CC-BY, NoAI, human
  likeness, voice, logo, or music assets into production without explicit
  policy rows.
- Reference-only assets cannot be treated as source-format art, retargeting
  source, mesh kit input, or generated derivative source unless the license row
  says the actual source format is available.
- Plugin descriptors and module descriptors are scanned on every package gate.
  Engine-version drift, missing modules, unsupported target platforms, and
  unexpected runtime dependencies are blockers or explicit waivers.
- Raw third-party actors should be wrapped by Ascentia profiles/adapters before
  gameplay systems depend on them. UDS is a weather provider, not the weather
  architecture; GASP is an animation provider, not the combat architecture.
- Upgrades are scenario-driven. A plugin or asset pack update needs expected
  changed files, regression scenarios, rollback stance, and owner sign-off.
- Contractor or generated content needs the same provenance records as Fab
  content, plus human-likeness/voice/trademark/music fields when relevant.
- Credits and attribution are not a last-week task. The policy writes credit
  line requirements as soon as content is accepted.

Designer tools:

```text
EUW_AscentiaExternalContentRegistry
EUW_AscentiaLicenseComplianceBoard
EUW_AscentiaPluginDescriptorDoctor
EUW_AscentiaExternalAssetQuarantine
EUW_AscentiaExternalUpgradePlanner
EUW_AscentiaAttributionAndCreditsBoard
EUW_AscentiaNoAIAndGeneratedContentAudit
EUW_AscentiaExternalContentPromotionGate
```

`EUW_AscentiaExternalContentRegistry` shows source rows, license policy,
purchase/version date, staging roots, production roots, owner, approval state,
and current shipped usage.

`EUW_AscentiaLicenseComplianceBoard` compares license policy to asset usage:
commercial project use, modification, collaborator sharing, source repository
storage, standalone redistribution risk, attribution, NoAI, reference-only
restriction, and shipping review state.

`EUW_AscentiaPluginDescriptorDoctor` reads `.uplugin` descriptors and expected
plugin profiles, then reports version drift, missing modules, unsupported target
platforms, unexpected dependencies, docs/support URLs, raw provider use, and
adapter coverage.

`EUW_AscentiaExternalAssetQuarantine` previews newly imported folders before
promotion: naming, folder layout, asset class, skeleton/material/LOD/collision
policy, license state, duplicate source, and dangerous references.

`EUW_AscentiaExternalUpgradePlanner` compares current and candidate external
versions and links required regression scenarios: GASP locomotion, Essential Bow
roll direction, UDS weather profiles, CommonUI shell, PCG/Landscry import,
Asset Factory mesh fit, and packaging.

Commandlets:

```text
AscentiaExternalContentAudit
  -Registry=/Game/Ascentia/Design/ContentGovernance/PDA_ExternalContentRegistry_MVP
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/ExternalContent

AscentiaLicenseComplianceAudit
  -Registry=/Game/Ascentia/Design/ContentGovernance/PDA_ExternalContentRegistry_MVP
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/LicenseCompliance

AscentiaPluginDescriptorAudit
  -ProfileRoot=/Game/Ascentia/Design/ContentGovernance/Plugins
  -PluginRoot=Plugins
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/PluginDescriptors

AscentiaExternalUpgradeAudit
  -UpgradePolicy=/Game/Ascentia/Design/ContentGovernance/PDA_ExternalUpgradePolicy_MVP
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/ExternalUpgrades

AscentiaAttributionAudit
  -Policy=/Game/Ascentia/Design/ContentGovernance/PDA_AttributionCreditPolicy_MVP
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/Attribution
```

Evidence:

```text
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/ExternalContent/{RunId}/source_report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/LicenseCompliance/{RunId}/license_report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/PluginDescriptors/{RunId}/plugin_report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/ExternalQuarantine/{RunId}/quarantine_report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/ExternalUpgrades/{RunId}/upgrade_report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/Attribution/{RunId}/credits_report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P23/GeneratedContent/{RunId}/generated_content_report.json
```

Proof matrix:

```text
ExternalContent_AllProductionAssetsHaveSource
ExternalContent_StagingOnlyBeforeApproval
License_FabStandard_NoStandaloneRedistributionRisk
License_CCBY_AttributionRowsGenerated
License_ReferenceOnly_NotUsedAsSourceFormat
PluginDescriptor_GASP_ProfileMatchesDescriptor
PluginDescriptor_UDS_ProfileMatchesDescriptor
PluginDescriptor_TargetPlatformsSupported
PluginDescriptor_RuntimeDependencyWaivers
Upgrade_EssentialBow_RollRegressionRequired
Upgrade_GASP_LocomotionRegressionRequired
Upgrade_UDS_WeatherScenarioRequired
```

P23V starter tickets:

- P23V-001: Add external content source, license policy, provenance, plugin
  profile, approval, and upgrade-plan structs.
- P23V-002: Add External Content Registry and License Compliance Board with
  Fab Standard, CC-BY, internal-generated, and reference-only starter policies.
- P23V-003: Add Plugin Descriptor Doctor using `.uplugin` and module descriptor
  metadata for GASP, Essential Bow, UDS, CommonUI, PCG, Python/MCP, Mover, and
  NetworkPrediction.
- P23V-004: Add External Asset Quarantine and promotion gate before staged
  folders can become production assets.
- P23V-005: Add External Upgrade Planner with regression scenario links for
  animation, atmosphere, UI, PCG, mesh generation, and package gates.
- P23V-006: Add Attribution/Credits Board and NoAI/generated-content audit.
- P23V-007: Add dashboard and package blockers for missing source records,
  missing license policies, unapproved production usage, plugin drift, and
  missing attribution.

Recommended sequencing:

1. Build P23V-001 through P23V-003 before importing or upgrading any more
   content packs.
2. Add P23V-004 before the next Fab/Marketplace kit is promoted into `/Game`.
3. Add P23V-005 before updating GASP, Essential Bow, UDS, or CommonUI.
4. Add P23V-006 before public screenshots, store pages, credits, soundtrack,
   voice, or contractor content broaden.
5. Add P23V-007 once the registry can block packaging safely.

written." Each phase should eventually receive its own expanded chapter.

- settings registry;

- title/load flow;
- localization pipeline;
- build/cook gates;
- crash/log hygiene.

Data:

- settings definitions;
- input glyph rows;
- localization tables;
- platform config.

Designer surface:

- title screen;
- settings UI;
- remap UI;
- credits/legal;
- accessibility UI.

Proof:

- packaged smoke;
- settings persistence;
- remap persistence;
- localization scan;
- validation commandlet clean.

### P22: Multiplayer

Write this phase as replication maturation, not a rewrite.

Native:

- ability prediction audit;
- authority audit;
- replicated inventory/equipment;
- world-state sync;
- co-op session flow;
- anti-cheat-sensitive paths.

Data:

- multiplayer rules;
- summon/invasion/co-op definitions if chosen;
- network test maps.

Designer surface:

- co-op UI;
- summon signs or matchmaking UI;
- multiplayer feedback cues.

Proof:

- listen server smoke;
- dedicated server smoke if supported;
- latency/prediction tests;
- inventory/equipment replication tests;
- combat desync tests.

## 25. Open Decisions

These need explicit calls before deep implementation:

- Content source authority:
  - CSV exported from xlsx is source of truth;
  - DataTables are edited directly in-editor;
  - PrimaryDataAssets own all content;
  - hybrid CSV for bulk values plus PrimaryDataAssets for object refs.
- Content registry route:
  - evolve `UAscentiaItemRegistrySubsystem` into the first resolved registry;
  - create a generalized `UAscentiaContentRegistrySubsystem`;
  - adopt UE Data Registry plugin after schemas stabilize;
  - keep per-system registries but enforce shared provenance/report contracts.
- Generated asset policy:
  - generated DataTables are tracked in git;
  - generated DataTables are reproducible artifacts and only CSV/scripts are
    tracked;
  - generated assets are tracked for milestone snapshots only;
  - production build pulls generated assets from an artifact store later.
- Fallback debt policy:
  - fallback allowed in all editor/dev builds but reported;
  - fallback blocks packaged builds;
  - fallback allowed only for tagged prototype systems;
  - fallback allowed per phase until the phase's proof gate closes.
- Tool registry source:
  - `PDA_ToolDescriptor` assets are the source of truth;
  - `DT_ToolDescriptors` owns the registry and DataAssets add rich references;
  - generated registry is built from Editor folder scans;
  - hybrid DataAsset source with generated dashboard/cache tables.
- Tool mutation strictness:
  - all mutating tools require manual approval forever;
  - trusted tools can auto-apply low-risk repairs after dry-run;
  - only commandlet/CI can auto-apply repairs;
  - no tool mutates production assets until its descriptor reaches
    `ValidatedMutation`.
- MCP automation authority:
  - one Ascentia editor instance may own MCP at a time;
  - MCP may inspect multiple editors but mutate only the locked project;
  - MCP mutation is disabled unless launched through `EUW_AscentiaMCPAutomationGuard`;
  - MCP is read-only until the editor session guard is proven stable.
- Third-party bridge acceptance:
  - bridge a provider only after an adapter and risk profile exist;
  - allow direct provider actors in prototype maps only;
  - block cooked builds when raw provider settings bypass Ascentia profiles;
  - accept direct provider use for art-only content but not gameplay contracts.
- Tool evidence retention:
  - keep all evidence indefinitely during MVP;
  - keep latest passing plus latest failing run per tool/phase;
  - archive evidence by milestone and prune local runs older than a threshold;
  - dashboard stores summaries while full artifacts move to an artifact store.
- P11 boss move selection:
  - phase-authored weighted matrix with range/angle/cooldown/repeat penalties;
  - Behavior Tree branches own move choice for MVP;
  - utility score service writes selected move to Blackboard;
  - bespoke boss planner only after the first boss roster proves the need.
- P11 phase transition policy:
  - health thresholds only;
  - health plus arena/world-state triggers;
  - phase entry can interrupt current move;
  - phase entry waits for a legal transition window.
- P11 fog wall and checkpoint policy:
  - fog wall closes only after boss aggro;
  - fog wall closes on arena entry volume;
  - nearest campsite owns respawn;
  - boss-adjacent respawn anchor is separate from campsite.
- P11 stagger/punish policy:
  - poise/stagger only;
  - parry/riposte only;
  - both poise stagger and deterministic punish windows;
  - no riposte until damage/build math stabilizes.
- P11 arena co-design strictness:
  - boss moves adapt to existing arena;
  - arena is built around boss move grammar;
  - both must pass a boss arena motion proof before phase gate closes.
- P11 target-lock ownership:
  - keep lock-on inside combat component for MVP only;
  - extract lock-on state to `UAscentiaTargetLockComponent`;
  - shared targeting subsystem owns candidate resolution for lock-on, ranged,
    throwables, spells, weak points, and AI-facing reuse;
  - Gameplay Targeting presets become the data-authoring route after the native
    resolver facade is stable.
- P11 combat camera route:
  - tune spring arm values directly on the pawn during prototype;
  - move free/lock-on/boss/ranged camera values into combat camera profiles;
  - future Gameplay Camera assets may consume the same profile data, but are not
    required for the first stable slice;
  - camera collision, wall slide, target framing, and accessibility scaling must
    produce evidence before camera comfort is marked green.
- P11 aim assist strictness:
  - no aim assist except hard lock-on;
  - gamepad-only friction/magnetism with mouse excluded by default;
  - per-profile aim assist values with accessibility scaling;
  - every ranged/throwable/spell profile must state whether assist, reticle, and
    target cycling are enabled.
- Accessibility acceptance route:
  - accessibility is an aspirational checklist only;
  - accessibility profiles and settings exist but do not block content;
  - text, contrast, input, subtitle, motion, flash, and store-claim evidence
    block milestone acceptance;
  - accessibility gates are integrated into every P20/P21 release package.
- Photosensitivity strictness:
  - warning splash only;
  - low-flash profile only;
  - VFX/UI/cinematic flash audit with blockers for policy failures;
  - VFX cannot enter milestone content until low-flash scaling and flash audit
    evidence exist.
- Inclusive input strictness:
  - only platform-level remapping;
  - in-game remapping for core gameplay only;
  - in-game remapping plus hold/repeat alternatives for all gameplay-critical
    actions;
  - remap, hold, repeat, prompt, tutorial, and glyph evidence required before
    release claims.
- P13 formula route:
  - keep current component-local formula for MVP only;
  - move armor/resistance/status/crit to a named GAS execution;
  - use native formula service plus GameplayEffect SetByCaller application;
  - keep formulas native but author every coefficient through profiles/curves.
- P13 formula trace policy:
  - trace every formula step in editor/dev builds;
  - trace only failed/out-of-band scenarios by default;
  - trace full steps only in formula lab, with runtime recording opt-in;
  - store compact trace summaries in telemetry and full traces in evidence.
- P13 true damage policy:
  - true damage only for executions and death/reset rules;
  - true damage allowed for selected boss mechanics with explicit UI/cue warning;
  - no player-facing true damage except deterministic execution profiles;
  - validator blocks true damage unless source tag is whitelisted.
- P13 defense model:
  - armor mitigates physical-family only;
  - armor mitigates all damage and resistances specialize by school;
  - armor remains physical-family for players but enemy/boss profiles can opt in
    to all-damage armor;
  - introduce an effective-survivability stat view before changing the formula.
- P13 status policy:
  - overflow retained vs cleared;
  - decay always on vs delayed decay;
  - refresh vs extend vs stack;
  - boss immunity windows after trigger;
  - cleanse/cure item timing and save/load persistence.
- P13 status save policy:
  - transient combat buildup never persists through reload;
  - active DOT/debuff states persist only if marked long-term;
  - world-state afflictions use separate save fragments and UI explanation;
  - boss/encounter status state resets with encounter reset policy.
- P13 critical policy:
  - random crits on all eligible hits;
  - random crits only on light/skill/spell categories;
  - deterministic backstab/riposte/stance-break only;
  - hybrid, with random crit modest and executions deterministic.
- P13 effective-survivability UI policy:
  - show only simple school defense summaries in player UI;
  - show effective health bands and wasted resistance caps in advanced UI;
  - keep full survivability math editor/debug only;
  - expose archetype comparison cards in character/build screens.
- P14 weapon action authoring route:
  - DataTable rows for all action definitions;
  - PrimaryDataAssets per weapon type/action map;
  - hybrid rows for numeric bulk plus assets for montage/cue/projectile refs;
  - GameplayAbility Blueprint subclasses per special action only.
- P14 weapon fallback policy:
  - native fallback allowed only in editor/dev builds and reported;
  - fallback allowed per phase while marked in the moveset matrix;
  - fallback blocks packaged phase gates;
  - fallback can ship only for intentionally generic unarmed/basic actions.
- P14 targeting/projectile route:
  - simple trace/projectile C++ for MVP;
  - Gameplay Targeting System presets for ranged/throwable/spell targeting;
  - hybrid Targeting presets for selection/filtering plus custom projectile
    actors for flight/impact;
  - defer data-driven targeting until lock-on and weak points are designed.
- P14 projectile authority:
  - server authoritative projectile spawn and hit application;
  - client cosmetic projectile plus server trace confirmation;
  - hitscan for MVP, projectile actors for production ranged identity;
  - per-profile delivery type, with validation for prediction/readability.
- P14 pairing rule strictness:
  - family-only dual wield/power stance;
  - explicit weapon-type pairs only;
  - item-authored exceptions;
  - loose pairing with weaker generic mixed movesets.
- P14 animation authority:
  - montages/notifies own all timing;
  - native action map owns timing and montages are presentation;
  - hybrid, where montage notifies author windows and native validators enforce
    required windows, cost, contact, and cancel policy.
- P14 notify authority:
  - gameplay notifies fire on authority and presentation notifies fire locally;
  - all notifies fire locally while server validates inferred windows;
  - damage/projectile/resource notifies are authoritative, cosmetic notifies are
    presentation-only;
  - no notify-driven gameplay in packaged builds unless manifest validation
    passes.
- P14 generated/marketplace weapon acceptance:
  - generated weapons remain staging until mount, collision, LOD, material, and
    provenance reports pass;
  - marketplace weapons can enter prototype folders immediately but block phase
    gate until normalized;
  - Asset Factory output may auto-create socket-profile drafts but Designer
    verdict is required before production rows reference them;
  - every accepted weapon stores source/provenance and license notes.
- P15 spell delivery route:
  - simple trace/projectile C++ for MVP;
  - Gameplay Targeting System presets for selection/filtering;
  - hybrid Targeting presets plus custom projectile/area actors;
  - one-off Blueprint delivery per spell only for prototypes.
- P15 spell commit timing:
  - validate target before Eth/cooldown for most spells;
  - spend Eth at cast start but apply cooldown only on release;
  - spend partial cost on cancel/interruption;
  - profile-driven commit policy per spell cast type.
- P15 spell fallback policy:
  - Ether Spark allowed as editor/dev fallback only;
  - Ether Spark can remain as intentional starting spell if fully authored;
  - fallback spell blocks packaged phase gate;
  - fallback spell appears in UI with explicit prototype marker.
- P15 Eth recovery policy:
  - no baseline regen, flask/camp/items only;
  - slow always-on regen;
  - delayed regen after spell use or damage;
  - exploration-only regen with combat suppression.
- P15 spell loadout model:
  - one equipped spell for MVP;
  - hotbar/quick slots;
  - attunement slots unlocked by Focus/progression;
  - weapon-bound spell slots for staves/foci/spellblade gear.
- P15 spell targeting policy:
  - free-aim first, lock-on assists only;
  - lock-on primary for projectile spells;
  - Gameplay Targeting presets own selection/filter/sort;
  - per-spell profile with settings-exposed aim assist.
- P15 summon policy:
  - no summons for MVP;
  - limited temporary companions only;
  - persistent companion system later;
  - enemy-only summons until player magic is deeper.
- P15 summon ownership policy:
  - summon damage credits owner for rewards and aggro;
  - summon has independent loot/aggro credit;
  - summons cannot receive loot credit, only combat support;
  - profile-driven per summon category.
- P15 flask allocation/upgrades:
  - fixed separate Health and Eth charge pools;
  - shared pool allocated at campsites;
  - upgrade tree with charge-count and restore-strength branches;
  - rare upgrades add secondary effects.
- P15 flask save/allocation policy:
  - allocation changes only at campsites;
  - allocation changes from menu anywhere outside combat;
  - upgrades immediately alter max charges but allocation waits for next camp;
  - Health/Eth flasks remain separate until shared allocation UI exists.
- P16 itemization axis:
  - pure affix;
  - sockets/gems;
  - set items;
  - uniques/aspects;
  - hybrid.
- P16 item mutation strictness:
  - deterministic upgrades only;
  - deterministic upgrades plus one crafted affix lane;
  - aspect/imprint extraction and application;
  - high-risk corruption/sanctify lane later.
- P16 crafting/upgrading stance:
  - deterministic upgrade tracks only;
  - deterministic plus narrow affix crafting;
  - high-risk final modification system later;
  - no item modification beyond drops until loot is deeper.
- P16 unique/legendary policy:
  - uniques only as authored boss/POI rewards;
  - legendary aspects as extracted/imprinted powers;
  - rare-like legendaries with one authored power;
  - defer legendary layer until affix/build math is stable.
- P16 economy sinks:
  - Stones only;
  - Stones plus crafting materials;
  - multiple region/boss currencies;
  - salvage-driven material economy.
- P16 buyback/stash policy:
  - no buyback;
  - short bounded buyback list;
  - buyback plus shared stash;
  - stash expansion as economy sink later.
- P16 item resolver source:
  - PrimaryDataAssets own base items and affixes;
  - DataTables own base items, affixes, and quality rules;
  - hybrid rows for numeric/bulk content plus PrimaryDataAssets for references,
    powers, presentation, and lore;
  - generated item sheets feed staging DataTables and block production until
    resolver reports pass.
- P16 roll determinism:
  - deterministic seed per item instance only;
  - deterministic seed per loot source plus per item instance;
  - server-authoritative deterministic seeds for all economy-affecting rolls;
  - prototype random allowed only in editor and reported as fallback debt.
- P16 transaction ledger strictness:
  - ledger only in debug builds;
  - ledger for crafting/vendor/salvage only;
  - ledger for every inventory/economy mutation in dev and proof builds;
  - compact ledger summaries persist in save data for meaningful mutations.
- P16 tooltip/report strictness:
  - UI may display unresolved item data during prototype;
  - tooltip must use resolver view models for equipped/vendor/crafting items;
  - all runtime item UI uses resolver view models before P16 gate closes;
  - packaged builds block on missing tooltip text keys, invalid compare groups,
    and unresolved modifier mappings.
- P16 reward pacing proof strictness:
  - manual playtest notes only;
  - loot distribution report per source category;
  - economy simulation report per world tier;
  - reward pacing report required for each accepted region, boss, and The Below
    reward profile.
- P16B The Below generation route:
  - authored pocket arena only for MVP;
  - deterministic room sequence from authored Level Instance room kits;
  - PCG decorates authored rooms but cannot create critical doors, exits, bosses,
    rewards, or save anchors;
  - every run writes a replayable plan before gameplay begins.
- P16B The Below reward/encounter authority:
  - direct cache grant for MVP only;
  - rewards always route through P16 loot source profiles and item transactions;
  - guardians and combat rooms always route through P18 encounter definitions;
  - modifiers can alter only declared tags, budgets, hazards, and presentation
    profiles.
- P17 world authoring route:
  - convert MVP_Arena-derived test world to World Partition;
  - create a fresh WP world and keep MVP_Arena as a test map.
- P17 Landscry/PCG promotion policy:
  - generated content can ship only after promotion to authored assets;
  - cosmetic PCG can remain generated if it has no save/gameplay state;
  - gameplay candidates from PCG become authored actors/assets before shipping;
  - Landscry output always enters staging before production promotion.
- P17 cartography source of truth:
  - runtime map scrapes placed actors and labels;
  - runtime map reads `PDA_WorldZone`, `PDA_POI`, map regions, discovery state,
    and marker definitions;
  - map, compass, guidance, NPC clues, and memory clues resolve from shared
    world-state tags;
  - no milestone map claim without baked map assets, marker coverage, save/load
    reveal proof, fast-travel safety, and accessibility evidence.
- P17 map reveal strictness:
  - reveal everything on first visit;
  - reveal broad regions plus authored POI/landmark discovery;
  - allow player pins and optional hidden memory clues;
  - never use the map as a checklist quest log unless design explicitly reverses
    the questless pillar.
- P17 population simulation route:
  - authored Actor-only ambient life for the first zone;
  - StateTree Actors for important ambient NPCs and wildlife;
  - Mass only for non-combat background populations;
  - no ambient population can create persistent gameplay state without stable
    source/persistence policy and P17/P18 handoff proof.
- P17 traversal strictness:
  - visual path only for MVP;
  - authored traversal route graph for every POI;
  - route graph plus fall recovery anchors and map hints;
  - full traversal proof including return routes, nav, resources, and save/load.
- P17 traversal verb strictness:
  - ad hoc Blueprint triggers for prototype-only traversal;
  - profile asset required for every verb before it gates progression;
  - profile plus anchor, camera, collision, save/restore, and nav-link parity
    proof before milestone route acceptance;
  - water/mount traversal remains prototype-only until route, save, AI, and
    performance evidence exists.
- P17 streaming travel policy:
  - fast travel teleports immediately and accepts streaming risk during prototype;
  - fast travel/respawn activates destination streaming source and waits;
  - shell loading screen owns presentation while world travel owns readiness;
  - packaged builds block on missing streaming-source proof.
- P17 world performance gate:
  - editor-only budget warnings;
  - packaged Development report required per zone;
  - performance and readability proof required before accepting any world zone.
- P17 zone delivery milestone strictness:
  - informal screenshots and Designer notes during concept only;
  - every blockout needs a named playable route and route evidence;
  - every ArtAlpha zone needs World Partition, nav, HLOD, PCG provenance,
    readability, and accessibility proof;
  - ReleaseCandidate zones require packaged route proof, Insights trace summary,
    no forbidden placeholders, and discipline signoff rows.
- P18 AI model:
  - BT/EQS only;
  - BT/EQS plus utility selection;
  - StateTree/Mass for selected populations;
  - full custom planner later.
- P18 perception model:
  - sight-only for MVP;
  - sight plus damage memory;
  - sight/hearing/damage/investigation for stealth-ready AI;
  - full faction/threat model now to support summons/co-op later.
- P18 boss move selection:
  - authored BT branches only;
  - utility-scored move matrix;
  - phase-authored weighted random with repeat penalties;
  - bespoke boss planner only for exceptional bosses.
- P18 encounter persistence:
  - all non-boss enemies respawn on rest;
  - unique elites persist defeated until world reset;
  - POI completion locks selected spawns permanently;
  - encounter data owns explicit reset/save policy per spawn group.
- P18 enemy definition source:
  - PrimaryDataAssets own all enemy definitions;
  - DataTables own enemy definitions and assets attach through lookup keys;
  - hybrid rows for bulk stats plus PrimaryDataAssets for behavior, ability, and
    presentation references;
  - generated enemy definitions from tooling enter staging until resolved reports
    pass.
- P18 threat target policy:
  - nearest visible hostile target only;
  - damage memory plus line-of-sight target switching;
  - full threat table for players, summons, companions, factions, and objectives;
  - boss-specific target policy overrides only when listed in the boss quality
    report.
- P18 EQS and slot reservation strictness:
  - advisory EQS only, no hard reservations;
  - hard reservations for melee slots only;
  - hard reservations for melee, flank, ranged, and boss anchors;
  - per-encounter strictness so prototype fights can warn while milestone fights
    block on conflicts.
- P18 encounter reset/save strictness:
  - reset all non-boss actors on rest and reload;
  - save unique elite and boss defeat only;
  - save explicit encounter fragments for POI completion, ambush discovery, and
    reward state;
  - block milestone encounters without a reset-policy preview and save/load
    proof.
- P18 boss quality gate strictness:
  - dashboard warning only during prototype;
  - boss cannot enter playtest without move matrix and phase proof;
  - boss cannot enter milestone without arena-motion, cue, persistence, and
    death-loop proof;
  - exceptional bosses require bespoke proof scenarios listed on the boss asset.
- P19 death loop:
  - Soulslike dropped currency;
  - softer corpse memory;
  - no drop penalty, only world reset/resource pressure.
- P19 character creator architecture:
  - Leader Pose modular character for first production creator;
  - Copy Pose for all modular parts that need physics/independent animation;
  - runtime Skeletal Mesh Merge for final optimized player avatars;
  - keep current VisualOverride costume path only as a temporary placeholder.
- P19 save migration strictness:
  - every save-version bump requires old-save fixtures;
  - fixtures only at milestone versions;
  - auto-default missing fields without fixtures during prototype;
  - block packaged builds when migration fixtures fail.
- P19 respec policy:
  - campsite respec with Stones cost;
  - rare item respec;
  - limited free respec during early access/prototype builds;
  - no respec until build math stabilizes.
- P19 NG+ reset policy:
  - reset bosses/POIs but retain map discovery;
  - reset map discovery and world state but retain character/inventory;
  - retain selected world-state achievements for alternate endings;
  - defer NG+ until first world zone and boss roster exist.
- P19 save IO policy:
  - synchronous saves remain acceptable for MVP maps only;
  - async save for campsite/menu writes, sync only for emergency shutdown;
  - async all production writes with snapshot locks and user-facing failure
    states;
  - platform-specific save policy selected through P21 package/profile data.
- P19 save fixture strictness:
  - fixtures only for milestone save versions;
  - fixture required for every save version bump;
  - fixture plus round-trip hash required for every save-fragment version bump;
  - packaged builds block on failed fixture migration.
- P19 death echo placement:
  - spawn at exact death transform when valid;
  - project to nearest nav-valid floor;
  - use authored death echo proxy volumes for boss arenas/falls/kill volumes;
  - always prefer last safe anchor if death location is streaming-unsafe.
- P19 creator/runtime separation:
  - creator selects pawn class directly;
  - creator saves identity/appearance while game mode chooses pawn class;
  - creator saves identity/appearance plus avatar assembly profile;
  - creator supports multiple runtime pawn archetypes only through validated
    character profile data.
- P19 avatar assembly tier:
  - Leader Pose only for first creator;
  - Leader Pose plus Copy Pose exceptions for physics-heavy parts;
  - runtime mesh merge for optimized final player avatars;
  - mesh merge only for NPC/crowd/player LODs until morph/customization needs are
    proven compatible.
- P19 creator cosmetic acceptance:
  - allow creator cosmetics once save tags exist, then fix issues manually;
  - require body/morph/groom/cloth/physics/portrait proof before promotion;
  - accept only body presets first, defer grooms and cloth until PhysicsAsset
    coverage passes;
  - require Avatar Cosmetic Board evidence for every milestone creator part.
- P20 cue routing authority:
  - GameplayAbilities fire GameplayCues directly;
  - gameplay systems emit presentation events and cue router resolves profiles;
  - hybrid, where GAS-owned cues route through GameplayCue and non-GAS events use
    presentation events;
  - all cue routes must be data-authored before P20 gate closes.
- P20 Niagara Data Channel threshold:
  - use ordinary Niagara components for all MVP effects;
  - use Data Channels only for impact/footstep/status burst events;
  - use Data Channels for every high-volume repeated burst family;
  - require a Data Channel profile whenever a cue can exceed budget in dense
    encounters.
- P20 audio architecture strictness:
  - keep `USoundBase` one-shots until content volume grows;
  - introduce audio cue profiles and concurrency groups first;
  - introduce MetaSound sources for parameterized whooshes, spells, ambience,
    and boss layers;
  - every combat/audio event needs a profile, concurrency policy, and settings
    scale before P20 gate closes.
- P20 audio mix/settings route:
  - direct SoundClass volume sliders for MVP;
  - Audio Modulation buses and mixes for production settings;
  - per-context mix profiles for combat, menu, dialogue, Below, and accessibility;
  - no milestone audio claim without bus/mix evidence and critical-cue clarity.
- P23 surface response authority:
  - let each Blueprint/notify choose its own sound/VFX/decal;
  - switch directly on Unreal physical surface enum in gameplay code;
  - map Physical Materials to Ascentia Gameplay Tags and resolve through surface
    response profiles;
  - no milestone world/combat surface claim without footstep, impact, weather,
    hazard, and accessibility evidence.
- P23 weather material update route:
  - per-actor Dynamic Material Instance edits from level Blueprints;
  - Material Parameter Collections for broad biome/weather state plus profile
    approved overrides for exceptional hero assets;
  - UDS/weather adapter writes only named profile parameters;
  - package blocks on unprofiled material parameter mutations.
- P17 atmosphere provider strictness:
  - UDS actors can be tuned directly in prototype maps only;
  - UDS can be used in production maps but every value is mirrored by an
    Ascentia atmosphere profile;
  - production maps may reference only `UAscentiaAtmosphereSubsystem` profiles
    and provider adapters, with raw provider debt blocking package promotion;
  - native Unreal sky actors replace UDS only behind the same provider interface.
- P17 weather gameplay strictness:
  - weather is presentation-only during MVP;
  - weather can affect materials and audio but not movement, AI, spawns, or
    stealth;
  - weather can affect gameplay only through `FAscentiaWeatherGameplayPolicy`
    rows with explicit opt-in tags and evidence;
  - milestone weather profiles block when they create implicit gameplay side
    effects.
- P20 accessibility scaling strictness:
  - global low/medium/high intensity only;
  - per-category scales for camera, rumble, flash, blood, trails, damage numbers,
    loot, and boss presentation;
  - per-profile accessibility group plus settings invariance proof;
  - no profile can enter milestone content without an accessibility group.
- P20 presentation performance gate:
  - editor PIE warnings only;
  - packaged arena smoke report required;
  - packaged report plus Unreal Insights trace required for milestone scenes;
  - dashboard blocks P20 status when presentation budget or UI invalidation
    reports fail.
- P20 photo mode shipping scope:
  - no player photo mode until post-MVP;
  - local clean screenshot only;
  - full photo mode with camera controls after shell, input, settings, and
    capture presets exist;
  - storefront/MRQ capture tooling remains separate from runtime photo mode.
- P20 share-safe capture policy:
  - evidence screenshots may include debug metadata;
  - player captures hide UI and debug data by default;
  - public share metadata excludes raw paths, account IDs, save IDs, and machine
    usernames;
  - spoiler states are blocked or marked evidence-only by profile.
- P12 world-state identity:
  - migrate current `FName` state flags to GameplayTags now;
  - keep `FName` runtime flags as MVP but validate against a registry asset;
  - split transient runtime flags from save-persistent world-state tags.
- P12 world-script shape:
  - condition/effect DataAssets only;
  - Blueprint node graphs with validated condition/effect nodes;
  - external narrative graph export/import later;
  - allow ad hoc Blueprint events only in prototype maps and report them as
    fallback debt.
- P12 interaction backend:
  - lightweight interactables only for MVP;
  - Smart Objects for shared AI/player activities;
  - hybrid where `PDA_InteractionDefinition` owns Ascentia rules and Smart
    Objects provide reservation/spatial slots.
- P12 dialogue shape:
  - row-driven linear/stateful dialogue only;
  - node graph for branching conversations;
  - external narrative tool export/import later.
- P12 character performance source:
  - facial/gesture tracks can live directly inside individual Level Sequences;
  - line IDs own facial solve, gesture, and scene-take bindings;
  - performance profiles choose audio-driven, video-driven, or hand-authored
    animation per line/character importance;
  - no performance-captured scene reaches milestone status without avatar
    binding, skip/restore, subtitle, camera, and accessibility evidence.
- P12 performance capture staging:
  - Take Recorder output can be edited in place;
  - Take Recorder output enters staging with source/capture metadata first;
  - approved takes promote through a scene-take policy with replacement risk;
  - Control Rig corrections need owning line/scene provenance and stale-correction
    warnings.
- P12 guidance strictness:
  - HUD objective tracker only;
  - NPC/map/memory hints only;
  - guidance subsystem resolves every surface from world state;
  - no traditional quest log unless design explicitly reverses the questless
    pillar.
- P12 tutorial timing policy:
  - fixed short popups;
  - settings-scaled duration;
  - replay/disable controls for all non-critical tutorials;
  - tutorials complete from observed player actions, not only display.
- P12 reputation visibility:
  - reputation is fully hidden and expressed only through NPC behavior;
  - broad ranks are visible while exact scores remain hidden;
  - exact scores are visible in debug/tools only;
  - milestone content must state whether each reputation row is player-visible,
    hidden, or tool-only.
- P12 crime/witness strictness:
  - all protected-NPC crimes affect reputation globally;
  - only witnessed or explicitly scripted crimes affect persistent reputation;
  - hidden crimes can create local NPC memory but no global bounty;
  - every crime policy must prove server authority, witness logic, forgiveness,
    and save/load before milestone use.
- P12 vendor/crafting boundary:
  - vendors own all services;
  - crafting stations own crafting and vendors only buy/sell;
  - service profiles unify vendors, crafting, respec, upgrades, and special
    interactions.
- P12 NPC schedule scope:
  - no schedules until world scale begins;
  - campsite-only schedule/fate profiles;
  - region/state/weather schedule profiles for important NPCs;
  - full ambient simulation deferred to Mass/StateTree population work.
- P21 shell approach:
  - CommonUI/CommonInput now;
  - native UMG shell now and CommonUI migration later;
  - title/settings/remap before character creation;
  - character creation before final shell polish.
- P21 build cadence:
  - package Development builds weekly;
  - package only at milestone gates;
  - introduce Gauntlet-style packaged smoke once P17 world scale begins.
- P21 settings persistence route:
  - `UGameUserSettings` bridge plus `UAscentiaUserSettingsSaveGame`;
  - custom subsystem only during prototype, then migrate to engine settings;
  - CommonUI settings plugin pattern if it fits the final shell.
- P21 input remap route:
  - Enhanced Input Player Mappable Input Configs as the source of presets;
  - custom `FAscentiaInputBindingOverride` profile for persistence/conflicts;
  - defer full remap UI until glyph/action data is validated.
- P21 localization timing:
  - pseudo-localization now;
  - English-only shipping data with string tables now;
  - full PO export/import and vendor workflow later.
- P21 package evidence cadence:
  - local `Tools/package_ascentia.ps1` evidence now;
  - Project Launcher/UAT profiles once package profiles stabilize;
  - Gauntlet-style packaged smoke when map and session flows are larger.
- P21 shell route strictness:
  - widgets may push themselves during prototype;
  - shell subsystem owns only title/pause/settings;
  - shell subsystem owns every title, pause, settings, save-slot, creator,
    loading, modal, and error transition;
  - packaged builds block on shell routes that bypass transition reporting.
- P21 settings transaction policy:
  - settings apply immediately and save on exit;
  - low-risk settings apply immediately while display settings use rollback;
  - every setting uses transaction result/reporting before P21 gate closes;
  - risky settings require preview, confirmation, rollback, and persistence
    proof.
- P21 localization proof cadence:
  - pseudo-localization screenshots only for shell screens;
  - pseudo-localization for shell plus inventory/loot/HUD;
  - pseudo-localization for every player-facing screen named in phase gates;
  - missing text keys block packaged profiles after localization targets exist.
- P21 support bundle privacy:
  - raw logs only in developer builds;
  - sanitized support bundles for packaged development;
  - user-visible report hides internal paths/tokens but preserves build,
    culture, input, shell, save, and failure metadata;
  - shipping bundles require explicit opt-in/export workflow.
- P21 platform provider boundary:
  - direct Steam/EOS calls allowed from gameplay during prototype;
  - `UAscentiaPlatformServiceSubsystem` owns all provider calls;
  - Null provider proof required before Steam/EOS profile work;
  - packaged builds block when gameplay modules hard-reference provider APIs.
- P21 achievement/stat authority:
  - gameplay systems unlock provider achievements directly;
  - gameplay emits durable progress facts and platform layer maps them;
  - achievements can read save facts but never mutate save or progression;
  - public achievement IDs require migration notes before release visibility.
- P21 cloud-save policy:
  - cloud every file under Saved by provider default;
  - cloud gameplay saves and slot summaries only;
  - exclude machine-specific settings such as video/device data;
  - cross-platform/demo-full sharing requires explicit policy and fixture proof.
- P21 player-trust/compliance strictness:
  - public claims can live in free text until store submission;
  - every public claim requires evidence before release candidate;
  - every rating, privacy, store, credit, and data-request claim has owner,
    freshness, locale coverage, and evidence;
  - no public demo, store page, trailer, press kit, or release candidate goes
    live without the compliance release gate.
- P21 provider rollout:
  - Steam first because PC storefront is likely first;
  - EOS first because cross-platform services stay portable;
  - Null provider first, then Steam prototype, then EOS prototype;
  - no provider becomes mandatory until release profile evidence exists.
- Tooling implementation route:
  - Editor Utility Widgets first;
  - `AscentiaEditor` C++ tools and details customizations where schema/undo needs
    stronger native support;
  - MCP/Python scripts for repeatable automation;
  - external desktop/web tools only when the workflow is larger than the UE
    editor should host.
- Tooling build/buy/bridge stance:
  - buy/bridge UDS, Landscry, Asset Factory, Skin Forge, and strong engine
    systems where they solve commodity problems;
  - build Ascentia adapters, profiles, validators, evidence, and Designer
    preview tools around them;
  - replace third-party providers only if profile/adapter seams cannot protect
    production goals.
- Tooling first sprint:
  - Build Doctor, Dependency Doctor, Motion Inspector, Notify Window Doctor,
    Weapon Mount Lab;
  - UDS Atmosphere Director before broad world lighting work;
  - World Director and Content Provenance Browser when Landscry/PCG scale begins.
- MCP/editor automation policy:
  - one Ascentia editor owns automation at a time;
  - scripts must verify project path, editor session, active PIE state, and dirty
    packages before mutating;
  - mutating automation needs dry-run and evidence output.
- P22 multiplayer target:
  - co-op only;
  - co-op plus invasions;
  - async ghosts/messages only;
  - deferred indefinitely but replication discipline retained.
- P22 server topology:
  - listen-server only;
  - listen-server first with dedicated-server proof later;
  - dedicated-server compatible from first multiplayer milestone;
  - no committed topology until network scenario reports prove the cost.
- P22 replication scaling route:
  - default UE replication only;
  - Replication Graph for large actor counts/world zones;
  - Iris pilot after replicated object schemas are stable;
  - choose RepGraph or Iris per milestone, not both as simultaneous production
    paths.
- P22 session provider:
  - Null subsystem for local proof only;
  - EOS as default PC online provider;
  - Steam as first PC storefront provider;
  - provider adapter selected after session UX and save/progression rules are
    written.
- P22 loot/progression ownership:
  - host-owned world loot with guest grants;
  - personal loot for co-op;
  - shared loot with pickup locks;
  - no guest persistent grants until exploit-sensitive paths are audited.
- P22 audit gate strictness:
  - warning-only authority matrix;
  - block multiplayer proof on unknown authority;
  - block packaged multiplayer profiles on unknown authority and missing server
    validation;
  - block any economy/save-affecting online feature until exploit review passes.
- P22 network scenario proof route:
  - PIE multiplayer checks are developer diagnostics only;
  - dry-run scenario/profile validation before launching multiple processes;
  - Gauntlet/UAT-style server/client runs required for milestone proof;
  - Networking Insights trace/log/screenshot artifacts required when debugging
    bandwidth, RPC, property, or relevancy failures.

16. Multiplayer future-proofing chapter:
    - first implementation contract captured in section 16;

## 27. Parallel Agent Execution Model

Ascentia can use multiple coding/data agents effectively, but only if the work
is split around stable contracts instead of broad phase names. A "P17 agent" and
a "P20 agent" can collide immediately if both invent evidence report shapes,
Gameplay Tags, folder conventions, or DataAsset ownership rules. A
`P17WorldPartitionProof` agent and a `P20AudioStateReport` agent can work in
parallel safely if both consume the same tool report writer, tag registry,
source-control policy, and evidence-path contract.

The right question is not "how many agents can type code?" It is "how many
agents can modify owned surfaces without forcing another agent to rebase,
reinterpret data, or repair Designer-facing assets?" With the current compendium
shape, the practical answer is:

- now, before the foundation/tooling spine exists: 4 to 6 high-trust agents;
- after TOOL-001 through TOOL-005 and FND-001 through FND-004 land: 8 to 10
  agents;
- after content registry, save schema, Designer surface registry, and evidence
  report contracts stabilize: 10 to 14 agents;
- above 14, add integration/review agents before adding feature agents.

### 27.1 Agent classes

Use clear agent classes, not generic "C++ worker" slots.

```text
Foundation contract agent
  owns Gameplay Tags, ability-set grants, attribute clamps, core validation
  shape, module boundaries, and coding standards.

Tooling spine agent
  owns UAscentiaToolSubsystem, descriptor assets, report writer, evidence
  browser, mutation gate, editor session guard, and dashboard export contracts.

Runtime system agent
  owns one gameplay subsystem plus its request/result structs, save fragment,
  telemetry rows, Blueprint-callable facade, tests, and evidence scenario.

Data/schema agent
  owns DataTables, PrimaryDataAssets, CSV/import manifests, validation rows,
  fallback-debt reports, provenance, and migration notes.

Designer surface agent
  owns EUWs, Details exposure, DataAsset defaults, commandlet wiring, report
  display, and "what Designers see" proof.

Content integration agent
  owns animation packs, assets, sockets, PhysicsAssets, materials, maps, Data
  Layers, external provenance, and promotion gates.

QA/evidence agent
  owns scenario profiles, smoke tests, report validation, packaged proof,
  dashboard status, and regression matrix wiring.

Integration lead agent
  owns merge order, API freeze windows, conflict review, cross-lane schema
  changes, and final acceptance of shared contracts.
```

The ideal lane is usually a pair: one runtime/data agent plus one
Designer/evidence agent. For example, P11 combat motion should not be "one agent
implements the solver and later someone makes it visible." It should be:

```text
Agent A: UAscentiaCombatMotionSolver, request/result structs, motion component,
         adapter interfaces, root-motion/floor/collision policy.
Agent B: PDA_CombatMotionProfile_*, EUW_AscentiaMotionInspector,
         dodge direction proof, notify-window audit, evidence report.
```

That pattern keeps C++ from becoming invisible machinery and keeps Designer
assets from drifting into unvalidated knobs.

### 27.2 Safe first wave

The first wave should be intentionally conservative:

```text
Agent 01 Foundation GAS/Tags
  FND-001 through FND-004

Agent 02 Tooling Spine
  TOOL-001 through TOOL-005

Agent 03 Combat Motion
  P11-001 through P11-003

Agent 04 Content Data Registry
  P23-001 through P23-004

Agent 05 UI/Settings/Glyphs
  UX-001 through UX-004

Agent 06 Save/Progression Spine
  P19-001 through P19-003
```

This is the highest-return burst because it builds the shared vocabulary other
agents need: tags, report shapes, content registry records, settings/glyph
interfaces, save fragments, and motion request/result patterns. Adding more
agents before these land mostly creates parallel uncertainty.

### 27.3 Second wave

After the first wave merges and runs clean evidence, scale to 8 to 10 agents:

```text
Agent 07 World Partition / Zone01
  P17-001 through P17-004

Agent 08 AI / Enemy Definitions
  P18-001 through P18-004

Agent 09 Presentation / Audio / VFX
  P20-001 through P20-004 or P20A-001 through P20A-003

Agent 10 Release QA / Pipeline
  PIPE-001 through PIPE-003 or P21Q-001 through P21Q-003
```

These can proceed once they consume rather than invent shared contracts. The
world agent uses the content registry and evidence writer. The AI agent consumes
Gameplay Tags and enemy definition schemas. The presentation agent consumes
cue/audio route contracts. The release agent consumes reports instead of
hand-parsing one-off logs.

### 27.4 Full production burst

After reports, registries, save fragments, and Designer surface governance are
stable, a 10 to 14 agent burst is realistic:

```text
01 Foundation/GAS/Tags
02 Tooling/Evidence Spine
03 Combat Motion/Boss Quality
04 Movesets/Weapons
05 Items/Economy
06 Magic/Resources
07 Save/Progression/Character Creation
08 World/Streaming/Traversal
09 AI/Encounters/Bosses
10 UI/Settings/Accessibility
11 Presentation/Audio/VFX/Performance
12 Data Pipeline/External Content
13 Release/Platform/Compliance
14 Integration Lead / Review Gate
```

This is the practical ceiling before communication overhead eats the gains.
Ascentia can temporarily exceed it for independent content-authoring tasks, but
core C++ and DataAsset schema work should stay below that ceiling unless a
dedicated integration lane is active.

### 27.5 Serialized contracts

These are not safe for parallel unsupervised edits. They need one owner at a
time, even if many agents consume them:

```text
Gameplay Tag taxonomy
Ability-set grant/removal structs
Attribute Set and GameplayEffect library policy
Save header, save fragment header, migration contract
Content registry header and provenance structs
Item instance identity and transaction ledger
Combat motion request/result structs
World actor identity/save record
Tool descriptor, report writer, evidence header
Designer surface registry
Settings registry and input glyph lookup contracts
Network authority/audit structs
Release gate result schema
Privacy data map and public-claim evidence schema
Folder/naming conventions for production assets
```

If a task needs to change one of these contracts, it becomes an integration
change. It should announce the schema change first, update downstream examples,
run the affected validators, and avoid merging simultaneous feature branches
that still target the old shape.

### 27.6 Parallel-safe lanes

These lanes are usually parallel-safe after their input contracts exist:

```text
EUW surfaces for already-defined report structs
Commandlets that only read assets and write reports
DataAsset default profiles inside owned folders
Scenario fixtures under owned evidence roots
Animation pack audits that do not rewrite shared retarget assets
World zone proof tools for one zone at a time
Audio/MetaSound parameter audits against fixed cue schemas
Accessibility audits that consume UI/settings contracts
Storefront/compliance audits that consume public-claim rows
External content provenance rows for staged, non-promoted assets
```

Parallel-safe does not mean review-free. It means the expected conflicts are in
owned assets, report output, or leaf tools rather than shared schema.

### 27.7 Branch and merge rhythm

Each agent branch should declare:

```text
Phase slice: P11-001, P23-002, TOOL-004, etc.
Owned roots: Source module folders, /Game/Ascentia/Design roots, Tools folder.
Shared contracts touched: none, or named explicitly.
Designer surface: EUW/DataAsset/Details rows added or changed.
Evidence root: Saved/AscentiaEvidence/... path.
Validation: commandlet, automation test, PIE proof, or manual checklist.
Rollback risk: save migration, asset migration, plugin change, map/content risk.
```

Merge order should prefer foundation before consumers:

```text
1. shared structs and enums
2. subsystem facades
3. DataAsset classes and default assets
4. validators/report writers
5. EUWs and Designer surfaces
6. content migration/promotion
7. dashboard/docs sync
```

If two branches both edit the same reflected type, DataAsset class, or content
folder convention, do not merge both by timestamp. Merge the contract branch,
refresh the second branch against it, then re-run its proof. Unreal reflection
and Blueprint defaults punish casual schema drift.

### 27.8 Integration agent duties

Once more than eight agents are active, one agent should do only integration:

- watch for shared-contract changes;
- run `rg` checks for stale type/tool/ticket names;
- review Build.cs/module dependency drift;
- check Blueprint exposure and Designer surface promises;
- run report-schema compatibility checks;
- keep phase docs and dashboard counts honest;
- reject hidden native-only features that lack a Designer surface;
- enforce source-control/OFPA/binary asset policy;
- summarize merge order and known blockers.

The integration agent should not be a spare feature agent. Its value is keeping
parallel work from quietly becoming incompatible work.

### 27.9 Recommended live operating mode

For the next real implementation burst, run six agents:

```text
Foundation/GAS
Tooling spine
Combat motion
Content data registry
UI/settings/glyphs
Save/progression
```

Once those produce green evidence, add four:

```text
World/Zone01
AI/enemies
Presentation/audio/VFX
Pipeline/release QA
```

Then add specialized lanes only when their dependencies are ready:

```text
P21 compliance after storefront/platform/release claims exist
P23 external content governance before more pack imports/upgrades
P17 atmosphere after World Partition zone profile exists
P20 performance after repeatable scenarios exist
P22 multiplayer after authority audits and single-player contracts are stable
```

In short: Ascentia can safely accelerate with 6 agents now, 10 agents soon, and
14 agents at mature production speed. Past that, add integration and review
capacity before adding more feature capacity.
